text: This video presents our approach to completion proctectomy with creation of an ilioanal jay pouch anastomosis and diverting loop ileostomy by single incision laparoscopic surgery. We have demonstrated our technique of superior mesenteric artery mobilization for tension-free pouch anastomosis.

The patient is a twenty-one year old female with medically refractory ulcerative colitis. She previously underwent subtotal colectomy with end ileostomy and this is the second of her three stage pouch surgeries.
Following informed consent, the patient was brought to the OR and IV induction followed by endotracheal intubation was performed. The patient was positioned in modified lithotomy.
The surgeon makes a circumferential incision around the end ileostomy site, fully mobilizing it from surrounding structures before temporarily closing and returning it to the abdomen.

The surgeon introduces a single port gel point device into the abdomen through the previous end ileostomy site. The rectum is identified, and a total mesorectal excision is performed by staying posterior throughout the procedure.

The surgeon takes down the lateral attachments and proceeds with anterior dissection. The uterus is retracted anteriorly to protect it from damage, providing better access to the pelvis. There is mild entry into the vaginal wall on the left side.

The surgeon laparoscopically repairs the defect using 3-0 Vicryl sutures in a figure-of-eight pattern. The dissection extends down to the pelvic floor, and the rectum is stapled off using a GIA-60 stapler. The completion of the procedure is confirmed by a bimanual examination.

The laparoscopic view shows the liver, gallbladder, right kidney, and duodenum in the upper abdomen. The surgeon navigates the challenge of switching orientation from the pelvis to the upper abdomen while maintaining exposure and effective instrument handling.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the superior mesenteric artery, the main vascular pedicle for the ileal pouch, ensuring it is protected to extend the ileal reach for anal anastomosis. The apex of the pouch is positioned to reach the inferior border of the pubic symphysis for a tension-free anastomosis.

The surgeon carefully separates the retroperitoneal attachments of the superior mesenteric artery to free the ileum. The superior mesenteric artery is mobilized down to the pancreas, allowing the ileum to be freed.

The surgeon creates a J-pouch by performing a side-to-side anastomosis using two fires of IL-A-hundred. The tip of the J-pouch is stapled off with TA-thirty and over-sewn. A leak test is conducted before introducing the J-pouch into the abdomen, followed by placing a purse string suture with two-zero proline. The EEA-thirty-one is used to create the pouch anal anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a pouchoscopy to assess for anastomotic leaks, which are found to be negative. A loop of bowel, located forty centimeters proximal to the pouch, is brought to the skin surface, and a stoma is matured in place. The resected rectal specimen is visible.

In summary, we performed a single incision laparoscopic completion protectomy with creation of an ilioanal j-pouch anastomosis and diverting loop ileostomy. Mobilization of the SMA by retroperitoneal incisions along its border is an effective way to accomplish attention-free ileal pouch anal anastomosis, and this can help minimize the need for permanent end ileostomy.

While single incision laparoscopic surgery requires a higher technical skill set and experience in comparison to conventional laparoscopic surgical techniques, this technique minimizes port site related complications and the risk of incisional hernia. Single incision laparoscopic surgery has the potential of improving cosmetic value while minimizing post-operative pain recovery time and improving the quality of life.
